Repo: /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay (branch harden/pass-3). It is a dataset-agnostic BLUEPRINT engine for rigorous empirical ML/NLP. Hard rules: the engine imports NO dataset/adapter specifics (ADR-0002); all engine computation/storage/observability + the bulk reasoning tier run on-box (ADR-0003) while the optional high-stakes tier is off-box by design; optional heavy backends are lazy-imported behind extras (ADR-0006).

This is the THIRD hardening pass. Passes 1 and 2 already fixed findings #101-#156 — read docs/hardening/PASS-1.md and docs/hardening/PASS-2.md and DO NOT re-report anything already fixed there. Use /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/.venv/bin/python and /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/.venv/bin/ruff if you want to run a PoC (read-only; do not commit). Be adversarial, precise, and concrete: every finding needs a real file:line, a crisp statement of the production-readiness defect, a PoC or mutation demonstrating it where possible, and a specific suggested fix. Prefer a few HIGH-confidence real defects over many speculative nits. If the area is genuinely clean, say so and return few/no findings.

YOUR DIMENSION: Methodology integrity (the scientific core): the two firewalls, pre-registration content-binding + timestamp ordering, the measurement↔interpretation fence, the three-verdict mapping and its statistics (empirical p, stability, tails), scorecard accounting, provenance hash-chain integrity. Look for ways a misimplemented adapter or adversarial input corrupts a verdict or the provenance.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

3	     2

8	     7	    the baseline builder is never handed an ``ExternalClaimsSource`` (its ``build`` signature
9	     8	    takes only a ``Corpus``) and that ``StudyDefinition`` refuses a claims source outside
10	     9	    adjudication mode. On top of that, :class:`ClaimBlindGuard` gives the claims source
11	    10	    *custody*: hand the source to the guard, and the only way to obtain it is
12	    11	    ``release()``/``access()``, which raise while the guard is sealed. Baseline construction
13	    12	    runs inside ``sealed()``; adjudication runs after it.
14	    13	
15	    14	    Caveat (audit pass 1, issue #1): the guard cannot police a baseline builder that ignores
16	    15	    it and reads external judgments smuggled into ``Corpus`` metadata/relations — that path
17	    16	    is closed by the adapter contract (see schema.py), not by this guard. The guard is
18	    17	    custodial defense-in-depth over the type-level separation, not the sole barrier.
19	    18	
20	    19	Firewall B — discover/confirm separation:
21	    20	    The data used to discover a hypothesis must not be the data used to confirm it.
22	    21	    :class:`DiscoverConfirmSplit` holds disjoint, non-empty discovery and confirmation id
23	    22	    sets and refuses to confirm on any id the discovery phase touched (or on an empty set).
24	    23	"""
25	    24	
26	    25	from __future__ import annotations
27	    26	
28	    27	from contextlib import contextmanager
29	    28	from dataclasses import dataclass
30	    29	from typing import Generic, Iterable, Iterator, TypeVar
31	    30	
32	    31	C = TypeVar("C")
33	    32	
34	    33	
35	    34	class FirewallViolation(RuntimeError):
36	    35	    """Raised when a methodological firewall is breached at runtime."""
37	    36	
38	    37	
39	    38	class ClaimBlindGuard(Generic[C]):
40	    39	    """Firewall A. While sealed, the held claims source is unreachable.
41	    40	
42	    41	    Custodial usage (preferred) — the guard owns the source, so the baseline pipeline holds
43	    42	    the guard rather than the source and cannot read claims while sealed::
44	    43	
45	    44	        guard = ClaimBlindGuard(claims_source)
46	    45	        with guard.sealed():
47	    46	            corpus = parser.parse(source)            # baseline build — claims unreachable
48	    47	            baseline = build_baseline(corpus, claim_guard=guard)
49	    48	        claims = guard.release()                      # adjudication — guard released
50	    49	
51	    50	    ``access(obj)`` remains for defensive checks at call sites that hold a reference directly:
52	    51	    it returns ``obj`` when unsealed and raises while sealed.
53	    52	    """
54	    53	
55	    54	    def __init__(self, claims_source: C | None = None) -> None:
56	    55	        self._sealed = False
57	    56	        self._claims_source = claims_source
58	    57	
59	    58	    @property
60	    59	    def is_sealed(self) -> bool:
61	    60	        return self._sealed
62	    61	
63	    62	    @contextmanager
64	    63	    def sealed(self) -> Iterator["ClaimBlindGuard[C]"]:
65	    64	        prior = self._sealed
66	    65	        self._sealed = True
67	    66	        try:
68	    67	            yield self
69	    68	        finally:
70	    69	            self._sealed = prior
71	    70	
72	    71	    def release(self) -> C:
73	    72	        """Return the custodial claims source, or raise while sealed / if none was given."""
74	    73	        if self._sealed:
75	    74	            raise FirewallViolation(
76	    75	                "Firewall A: external claims released during blind baseline construction"
77	    76	            )

79	    78	            raise FirewallViolation("Firewall A: no claims source is under this guard's custody")
80	    79	        return self._claims_source
81	    80

147	########## methodology/preregistration.py ##########
148	     1	"""Pre-registration integrity (METHODOLOGY.md §3, GOVERNANCE.md §2).
149	     2	
150	     3	GOVERNANCE §2 defines pre-registration as three steps: a hypothesis is (1) made specific and
151	     4	typed, (2) **content-hashed and committed**, and (3) **timestamped against a trusted authority**
152	     5	— *before* any confirmatory test. Steps 2–3 were a presence sentinel until now: ``Hypothesis.locked``
153	     6	asserted only that ``locked_at`` and ``timestamp_proof`` were non-``None``, so a hand-written
154	     7	``timestamp_proof="rfc3161:demo"`` "locked" a hypothesis and nothing stopped a study from
155	     8	locking *after* it had seen the baseline, or from swapping the hypothesis content after locking
156	     9	(audit pass 1, issue #6). Both firewalls (:mod:`adjudication`, :mod:`discovery`) and all of
157	    10	:mod:`confirm` rest on the lock, so a sentinel lock made the three verdicts decorative.
158	    11	
159	    12	This module makes the lock mean what the method says, by construction:
160	    13	
161	    14	- **Content binding** — :func:`canonical_hypothesis_digest` is a type-faithful SHA-256 over a
162	    15	  hypothesis's *decision-bearing* fields (statement, kind, origin, test, decision rule,
163	    16	  predicted direction, source claim, params). A valid proof must attest to *this* digest, so
164	    17	  the content the verdict is about is provably the content that was locked: change the claim,
165	    18	  the test, or the decision rule after locking and verification fails.
166	    19	- **Verifiable timestamp** — a pluggable :class:`TimestampAuthority` returns a
167	    20	  :class:`VerifiedTimestamp` only if the proof genuinely binds the digest. The engine ships one
168	    21	  real, data-sovereign authority (:class:`LocalHmacAuthority`, on-box HMAC) and the *Protocol*
169	    22	  so a study can plug an RFC-3161 TSA. It ships **no** silent-accept authority.
170	    23	- **Lock before confirm** — :func:`require_preregistered` rejects a lock whose attested time is
171	    24	  not strictly before the confirmation moment, so a hypothesis cannot be "pre"-registered after
172	    25	  the result is known.
173	    26	
174	    27	Honest scope (the lesson carried from ADR-0008): :class:`LocalHmacAuthority` proves content
175	    28	integrity, time binding, and unforgeability *relative to a secret that lives on this box*.
176	    29	Whoever holds that secret could backdate a proof — it is local tamper-evidence, **not**
177	    30	third-party non-repudiation. A study that needs non-repudiation wires an RFC-3161 authority
178	    31	(which sends only the digest, never the data, so it stays compatible with data sovereignty —
179	    32	ADR-0003 — at the cost of an external dependency the study opts into). And content binding
180	    33	covers the *structured fields*: free text inside ``statement``/``params`` is hashed (so it
181	    34	cannot change after locking) but the engine cannot judge whether that text is *meaningful* —
182	    35	that remains a review obligation (GOVERNANCE §2 "timestamped adaptive design").
183	    36	"""
184	    37	
185	    38	from __future__ import annotations
186	    39	
187	    40	import datetime as _dt
188	    41	import hmac
189	    42	from dataclasses import dataclass, replace
190	    43	from typing import Protocol

277	   130	class LocalHmacAuthority:
278	   131	    """A real, on-box timestamp authority binding a digest and an instant under a secret key.
279	   132	
280	   133	    ``stamp`` issues ``local-hmac:v1:<instant-iso>|<hmac-hex>`` where the MAC is
281	   134	    ``HMAC-SHA256(secret, digest || instant)``. ``verify`` recomputes the MAC over the digest
282	   135	    the engine derived from the *current* hypothesis and the instant carried in the proof, and
283	   136	    constant-time compares. The proof therefore verifies iff the content is unchanged, the
284	   137	    instant is unchanged, and the same secret is used — without the secret it cannot be forged
285	   138	    for arbitrary content or time.
286	   139	
287	   140	    Honest scope: this is local tamper-evidence, not third-party non-repudiation — the holder of
288	   141	    ``secret`` could backdate (see module docstring). Use an RFC-3161 authority when you need a
289	   142	    third party to vouch for the time.
290	   143	    """
291	   144	
292	   145	    authority_id = "local-hmac"
293	   146	
294	   147	    def __init__(self, secret: bytes) -> None:
295	   148	        if not isinstance(secret, (bytes, bytearray)) or len(secret) < 16:
296	   149	            raise ValueError("LocalHmacAuthority secret must be >= 16 bytes of key material")
297	   150	        self._secret = bytes(secret)
298	   151	
299	   152	    def _mac(self, digest: str, instant_iso: str) -> str:
300	   153	        msg = canonical_json(["local-hmac:v1", digest, instant_iso]).encode("utf-8")
301	   154	        return hmac.new(self._secret, msg, "sha256").hexdigest()
302	   155	
303	   156	    def stamp(self, digest: str, *, instant: _dt.datetime | None = None) -> str:
304	   157	        when = instant if instant is not None else _dt.datetime.now(tz=_UTC)
305	   158	        when = _require_aware_utc(when, "stamp instant")
306	   159	        instant_iso = when.isoformat()
307	   160	        return f"{_PROOF_PREFIX}{instant_iso}{_PROOF_FIELD_SEP}{self._mac(digest, instant_iso)}"
308	   161	
309	   162	    def verify(self, digest: str, proof: str) -> VerifiedTimestamp:
310	   163	        if not proof.startswith(_PROOF_PREFIX):
311	   164	            raise PreRegistrationError(
312	   165	                f"proof is not a {self.authority_id} v1 token (wrong prefix)"
313	   166	            )
314	   167	        body = proof[len(_PROOF_PREFIX) :]
315	   168	        instant_iso, sep, mac_hex = body.partition(_PROOF_FIELD_SEP)
316	   169	        if not sep or not instant_iso or not mac_hex:
317	   170	            raise PreRegistrationError("malformed local-hmac proof (expected <instant>|<mac>)")
318	   171	        # The MAC field must be exactly the hex of a SHA-256 digest. Validating its shape here
319	   172	        # (a) rejects garbage early and (b) keeps non-ASCII/non-hex out of hmac.compare_digest,
320	   173	        # which raises TypeError on non-ASCII str rather than returning False (#81).
321	   174	        if len(mac_hex) != 64 or any(c not in "0123456789abcdef" for c in mac_hex):
322	   175	            raise PreRegistrationError("malformed local-hmac proof (mac is not 64 lowercase hex)")
323	   176	        try:
324	   177	            parsed = _dt.datetime.fromisoformat(instant_iso)
325	   178	        except ValueError:
326	   179	            raise PreRegistrationError(f"proof instant is not ISO-8601: {instant_iso!r}") from None
327	   180	        instant = _require_aware_utc(parsed, "proof instant")
328	   181	        expected = self._mac(digest, instant_iso)
329	   182	        if not hmac.compare_digest(expected, mac_hex):
330	   183	            # Either the content (digest) changed since locking, the instant was tampered, or the
331	   184	            # proof was issued under a different secret. All are pre-registration failures.
332	   185	            raise PreRegistrationError(
333	   186	                "proof does not bind this hypothesis's content at the stated time "
334	   187	                "(content changed after locking, or proof was forged/issued by another authority)"
335	   188	            )

429	     1	"""The measurement ↔ interpretation fence (METHODOLOGY.md §6).
430	     2	
431	     3	A hard boundary separates **measurement** (numbers the engine produces) from
432	     4	**interpretation** (what they mean). Interpretation lives strictly downstream and cannot
433	     5	feed back into measurement. Any use of judgment — including LLM-assisted classification — is
434	     6	interpretation, and is labeled as such.
435	     7	
436	     8	These wrappers make the boundary explicit and type-visible in the pipeline. ``fence`` turns
437	     9	a measurement into an interpretation; there is deliberately no inverse — you cannot turn an
438	    10	interpretation back into a measurement.
439	    11	"""
440	    12	
441	    13	from __future__ import annotations
442	    14	
443	    15	from collections.abc import Mapping as AbcMapping
444	    16	from dataclasses import dataclass, field
445	    17	from typing import Any, Generic, Mapping, TypeVar
446	    18	
447	    19	from assay_engine._frozen import duck_mapping_items, freeze, freeze_mapping
448	    20	
449	    21	T = TypeVar("T")
450	    22	
451	    23	
452	    24	@dataclass(frozen=True, slots=True)
453	    25	class Interpretation(Generic[T]):
454	    26	    """A judgment *about* one or more measurements. Downstream-only; never an input upstream."""
455	    27	
456	    28	    value: T
457	    29	    basis: tuple[str, ...]  # refs (producer:inputs_hash) of the measurements this interprets
458	    30	    rationale: str
459	    31	    judged_by: str  # e.g. "operator" or a model identifier — judgment is always labeled
460	    32	    metadata: Mapping[str, Any] = field(default_factory=dict)
461	    33	
462	    34	    def __post_init__(self) -> None:
463	    35	        # Freeze the payload so the frozen record is deeply immutable + hashable (issue #29).
464	    36	        object.__setattr__(self, "value", freeze(self.value))
465	    37	        object.__setattr__(self, "metadata", freeze_mapping(self.metadata))
466	    38	
467	    39	
468	    40	def _contains_interpretation(obj: Any) -> bool:
469	    41	    """True if ``obj`` is, or recursively contains, an :class:`Interpretation`."""
470	    42	    if isinstance(obj, Interpretation):
471	    43	        return True
472	    44	    if isinstance(obj, AbcMapping):
473	    45	        # scan KEYS as well as values — an Interpretation used as a dict key would otherwise be
474	    46	        # smuggled into a Measurement undetected (#115)
475	    47	        return any(
476	    48	            _contains_interpretation(k) or _contains_interpretation(v) for k, v in obj.items()
477	    49	        )
478	    50	    duck = duck_mapping_items(obj)
479	    51	    if duck is not None:  # lazy/proxy dict-like — scan it like a mapping, not an opaque leaf (#140)
480	    52	        return any(_contains_interpretation(k) or _contains_interpretation(v) for k, v in duck)
481	    53	    if isinstance(obj, (list, tuple, set, frozenset)):
482	    54	        return any(_contains_interpretation(v) for v in obj)
483	    55	    return False

486	    58	@dataclass(frozen=True, slots=True)
487	    59	class Measurement(Generic[T]):
488	    60	    """A value produced purely by the engine from data — no judgment involved."""
489	    61	
490	    62	    value: T
491	    63	    produced_by: str
492	    64	    inputs_hash: str
493	    65	    metadata: Mapping[str, Any] = field(default_factory=dict)
494	    66	
495	    67	    def __post_init__(self) -> None:
496	    68	        # Structurally close the one-way fence: an interpretation may never re-enter as a
497	    69	        # measurement value — at the top level OR nested in a container/metadata (issues #22,
498	    70	        # #30). Note: ref/inputs_hash integrity (forgery resistance) is delegated to the
499	    71	        # provenance/audit layer, not enforced here.

501	    73	            raise TypeError(

503	    75	                "interpretation must not feed back into measurement (the fence)"

505	    77	        # Freeze the payload so the frozen record is deeply immutable + hashable (issue #29).
506	    78	        object.__setattr__(self, "value", freeze(self.value))
507	    79	        object.__setattr__(self, "metadata", freeze_mapping(self.metadata))

509	    81	    @property

515	    87	def fence(
516	    88	    measurement: Measurement[Any], value: T, *, rationale: str, judged_by: str

522	    94	    producer (audit pass 1, issue #16).
523	    95	    """

525	    97	        value=value,
526	    98	        basis=(measurement.ref,),
527	    99	        rationale=rationale,
528	   100	        judged_by=judged_by,
529	   101	        metadata={"inputs_hash": measurement.inputs_hash},
530	   102	    )

601	     1	"""Adjudication runner — the engine owns the firewall guarantee, not each study (ADR-0008).
602	     2	
603	     3	METHODOLOGY.md describes the adjudication pipeline: build a baseline *blind* to the external
604	     4	claims (Firewall A), convert each claim into a typed, pre-stated hypothesis, test each against
605	     5	the blind baseline, and *score the external source* against the validated baseline (§5). Until
606	     6	now the engine shipped the parts (a custodial :class:`ClaimBlindGuard`, the confirmatory tests,
607	     7	the verdicts) but no composition — so Firewall A held only if a study wired it correctly by
608	     8	hand. For a blueprint inherited by many studies, that pushes the single most important
609	     9	methodological guarantee onto every cloner, where it is easy to get silently wrong (build the
610	    10	baseline with the claims in scope and nothing stops you).
611	    11	
612	    12	:func:`adjudicate` makes the guarantee structural: it builds the baseline *inside* a sealed
613	    13	claim-guard that holds the claims source, so the baseline builder cannot reach the claims
614	    14	during construction (any attempt raises ``FirewallViolation``); only after the baseline is
615	    15	built does the runner release the claims and adjudicate. It then aggregates the verdicts into a
616	    16	:class:`SourceScorecard` — the §5 "score the source" step, expressed as alignment *frequency*
617	    17	on decisive claims, explicitly NOT a normative judgement of the source's quality (richer
618	    18	dimensions — where it aligns, diverges, leaves gaps — are data-surfaced downstream, per §5).
619	    19	"""
620	    20

721	   121	       running the builder in a separate process, which is out of scope. The guarantee is

773	   173	def adjudicate_with_baseline(
774	   174	    baseline: BaselineArtifact,
775	   175	    claims: "Iterable[ClaimRecord]",
776	   176	    *,
777	   177	    hypothesis_for: Callable[[ClaimRecord], Hypothesis],
778	   178	    confirm: ClaimConfirmer,
779	   179	    authority: TimestampAuthority,
780	   180	    not_after: _dt.datetime,

783	   183	) -> SourceScorecard:
784	   184	    """Adjudicate a **materialized claim set** against an already-built blind baseline (core).
785	   185	
786	   186	    Factored out of :func:`adjudicate` so a composed pipeline that builds the baseline once
787	   187	    (and shares it with discovery) can reuse the identical, hardened Firewall-A loop instead of
788	   188	    rebuilding the baseline. The caller is responsible for having built ``baseline`` blind and
789	   189	    for passing the ``not_after`` ordering instant captured *before* that build — so the
790	   190	    lock-before-baseline guarantee is preserved across the shared-baseline composition.
791	   191	
792	   192	    ``claims`` is **materialized once** here (not re-pulled from a source): a caller that gated
793	   193	    or recorded a claim snapshot must adjudicate *that* snapshot, not a set a re-iterable source
794	   194	    could change between the gate and the scoring (adversarial review #95). Enforces the same
795	   195	    claim↔hypothesis↔verdict identity + pre-registration checks as :func:`adjudicate`.
796	   196	    """
797	   197	    verdicts: list[Verdict] = []
798	   198	    materialized = list(claims)  # claims used only after the blind baseline is built
799	   199	    # within-run identity uniqueness: a repeated claim_id would be counted once per occurrence in
800	   200	    # _score, inflating the scorecard denominator and biasing alignment_rate (#138).
801	   201	    seen_claim_ids: set[str] = set()
802	   202	    for c in materialized:
803	   203	        if c.claim_id in seen_claim_ids:
804	   204	            raise FirewallViolation(
805	   205	                f"duplicate claim_id {c.claim_id!r} within one adjudication run — claim identity "
806	   206	                "must be unique (a repeat would inflate the scorecard denominator)"
807	   207	            )
808	   208	        seen_claim_ids.add(c.claim_id)
809	   209	    for claim in materialized:
810	   210	        hypothesis = hypothesis_for(claim)
811	   211	        if hypothesis.origin is not HypothesisOrigin.EXTERNAL_CLAIM:
812	   212	            raise FirewallViolation(
813	   213	                f"claim {claim.claim_id!r} produced a {hypothesis.origin.value!r} hypothesis; "
814	   214	                "adjudicated claims must be EXTERNAL_CLAIM (pre-stated, not data-surfaced)"
815	   215	            )
816	   216	        if hypothesis.source_claim_id != claim.claim_id:
817	   217	            raise FirewallViolation(
818	   218	                f"hypothesis for claim {claim.claim_id!r} carries source_claim_id "
819	   219	                f"{hypothesis.source_claim_id!r} — claim↔hypothesis misattribution"
820	   220	            )
821	   221	        # Pre-registration: the proof must bind THIS hypothesis's content (no post-lock content
822	   222	        # swap) and its attested lock time must precede the baseline build (so the claim-derived
823	   223	        # hypothesis cannot have been tuned to the baseline).
824	   224	        require_preregistered(hypothesis, authority=authority, not_after=not_after)
825	   225	        verdict = confirm(hypothesis, baseline, claim)
826	   226	        if verdict.hypothesis_id != hypothesis.hypothesis_id:
827	   227	            raise FirewallViolation(
828	   228	                f"verdict for hypothesis {hypothesis.hypothesis_id!r} reports hypothesis_id "
829	   229	                f"{verdict.hypothesis_id!r} — hypothesis↔verdict misattribution"
830	   230	            )
831	   231	        verdicts.append(verdict)
832	   232	        if on_step is not None:
833	   233	            # per-claim hook (in order) so a caller can record the pre-registered hypothesis AND
834	   234	            # its verdict to provenance as each happens, not in a post-hoc batch (#93).
835	   235	            on_step(hypothesis, verdict)
836	   236	
837	   237	    return _score(source_name, verdicts)

843	     5

847	     9	  require stability across resamples.

850	    12	are supplied by callers via the baseline toolkit; the engine's responsibility here is to
851	    13	refuse to confirm an unlocked hypothesis or on discovery data, validate the supplied
852	    14	statistics, and map an outcome onto one of the three verdicts honestly (including
853	    15	``indeterminate``).

856	    18	particular tail of the null. The empirical p-value is computed against *that* tail, so a
857	    19	claim predicting a low value is confirmable, and a genuine contradiction (significant in the
858	    20	opposite tail) is detectable — both were impossible under the previous direction-blind

1006	   168	def _empirical_p(null: Sequence[float], observed: float, tail: Direction) -> float:
1007	   169	    """One-sided empirical p-value with +1 smoothing for the given tail."""
1008	   170	    n = len(null)
1009	   171	    if tail == "greater":
1010	   172	        extreme = sum(1 for v in null if v >= observed)
1011	   173	    else:
1012	   174	        extreme = sum(1 for v in null if v <= observed)
1013	   175	    return (extreme + 1) / (n + 1)
1014	   176

1016	   178	def _resample_stability(
1017	   179	    resamples: Sequence[float], null: Sequence[float], tail: Direction, alpha: float
1018	   180	) -> float:
1019	   181	    """Fraction of resamples that **reproduce the observed effect**.
1020	   182	
1021	   183	    Each resample is scored as its own observed statistic against the null and must itself be
1022	   184	    significant in the predicted tail (empirical p ≤ ``alpha``). This measures reproduction of
1023	   185	    the observed *effect* — not merely landing on the predicted side of the null center, which a
1024	   186	    resample distribution clearing the null median by an epsilon would satisfy while reproducing
1025	   187	    nothing of the observed magnitude (audit pass 2, #139). The closer to 1.0, the more
1026	   188	    consistently the effect's significance reproduces across resamples.
1027	   189	    """
1028	   190	    significant = sum(1 for r in resamples if _empirical_p(null, r, tail) <= alpha)
1029	   191	    return significant / len(resamples)

########## methodology/discovery.py ##########
     1	"""Discovery runner — Firewall B (discover/confirm separation) enforced by construction.
     2	
     3	The mirror of :mod:`assay_engine.methodology.adjudication`. In discovery mode the data surfaces
     4	candidate hypotheses; Firewall B (METHODOLOGY.md §2, ADR-0005) requires that the data used to
     5	*discover* a hypothesis is not the data used to *confirm* it — otherwise discovering a pattern
     6	and "testing" it on the same data proves nothing.
     7	
     8	The engine had the split primitive (:class:`DiscoverConfirmSplit`) and a confirm step that
     9	refuses discovery ids, but no composition — so the separation held only if a study wired it by
    10	hand (same risk Firewall A had before ADR-0008). :func:`discover_and_confirm` makes it
    11	structural: it hands the ``discover`` step a corpus containing *only* the discovery partition
    12	and the ``confirm`` step a corpus containing *only* the held-out partition, so neither step can
    13	accidentally use the other's data.
    14	
    15	Honest scope (lesson from the adjudication review, ADR-0008): the engine partitions the
    16	**structural** data — units, and relations whose *both* endpoints are in the partition. It
    17	does NOT carry the corpus's free-form ``metadata`` into a subset (an arbitrary mapping cannot
    18	be partitioned safely — a per-unit index in metadata would otherwise leak the other partition
    19	wholesale), and it cannot police free-text inside ``Unit``/``Relation.attributes`` that an
    20	adapter might use to reference units outside the partition. So: the structural separation is
    21	enforced; keeping per-unit data out of metadata/attribute *payloads* is the adapter's
    22	obligation (the Firewall-B analogue of the corpus-content caveat in ADR-0005). And, as with
    23	adjudication, this is a *signature-level* guarantee — it prevents accidental double-dipping,
    24	not a step that willfully reflects into the caller's frame (only process isolation could).
    25	"""
    26	
    27	from __future__ import annotations
    28	
    29	import datetime as _dt
    30	from typing import Callable, Iterable, Protocol
    31	
    32	from assay_engine.contracts.schema import Corpus
    33	from assay_engine.methodology.firewalls import DiscoverConfirmSplit, FirewallViolation
    34	from assay_engine.methodology.hypothesis import Hypothesis, HypothesisOrigin
    35	from assay_engine.methodology.preregistration import (
    36	    TimestampAuthority,
    37	    require_preregistered,
    38	)
    39	from assay_engine.methodology.verdict import Verdict
    40

    42	class HeldOutConfirmer(Protocol):
    43	    """Study-supplied: confirm one locked, data-surfaced hypothesis on the held-out corpus."""

    48	def subset_corpus(corpus: Corpus, ids: Iterable[str]) -> Corpus:
    49	    """Return the sub-corpus of ``corpus`` restricted to ``ids``.
    50

    52	    ``ids`` (a relation to a unit outside the partition would leak that unit's existence/links
    53	    into the partition).
    54	
    55	    Corpus-level ``metadata`` is deliberately **dropped**, not carried through: it is an
    56	    arbitrary mapping the engine cannot partition safely. A per-unit index living in metadata
    57	    (e.g. ``{"by_unit": {unit_id: ...}}``) would otherwise hand the discovery step the held-out
    58	    partition wholesale — silently defeating Firewall B. Adapters that need partition-scoped
    59	    side data must put it on the ``Unit``/``Relation`` records (which *are* partitioned), not in
    60	    corpus metadata; free-text inside ``attributes`` that references foreign units remains the
    61	    adapter's obligation (ADR-0005 / ADR-0008 honest-scope caveat).
    62	    """
    63	    keep = frozenset(ids)
    64	    units = tuple(u for u in corpus.units if u.unit_id in keep)
    65	    relations = tuple(r for r in corpus.relations if r.source_id in keep and r.target_id in keep)
    66	    return Corpus(units=units, relations=relations)
    67

    69	def discover_and_confirm(
    70	    corpus: Corpus,
    71	    split: DiscoverConfirmSplit,
    72	    *,
    73	    discover: Callable[[Corpus], Iterable[Hypothesis]],
    74	    confirm: HeldOutConfirmer,
    75	    authority: TimestampAuthority,
    76	) -> list[Verdict]:
    77	    """Run discovery then confirmation with the data separation enforced by construction.
    78	
    79	    1. ``discover`` is handed a corpus containing only the discovery partition and returns
    80	       candidate hypotheses. They must be ``DISCOVERY``-origin and genuinely **pre-registered**
    81	       before confirmation — ``require_preregistered`` checks (via ``authority``) that the proof
    82	       binds each hypothesis's content and that its attested lock time precedes confirmation.
    83	       Discovering and confirming on the same data, or confirming a hypothesis whose content was
    84	       changed after locking, is the double-dipping Firewall B exists to prevent.
    85	    2. ``confirm`` is handed a corpus containing only the held-out partition and tests each
    86	       hypothesis there; the verdict must report the hypothesis it answers.
    87	
    88	    ``authority`` is the pre-registration timestamp authority (e.g.
    89	    :class:`~assay_engine.methodology.preregistration.LocalHmacAuthority`, or an RFC-3161
    90	    adapter); the engine ships no silent-accept default. A study's ``discover`` step locks each
    91	    discovered hypothesis (e.g. via :func:`~assay_engine.methodology.preregistration.
    92	    lock_hypothesis`) before returning it.
    93	
    94	    Returns the verdicts. Raises ``FirewallViolation`` (incl. ``PreRegistrationError``) on an
    95	    empty discovery or held-out partition (split ids that select no corpus units — a vacuous
    96	    test), a non-``DISCOVERY`` hypothesis, a hypothesis that is not verifiably pre-registered
    97	    before confirmation, or a verdict↔hypothesis misattribution.
    98	    """
    99	    discovery_corpus = subset_corpus(corpus, split.discovery_ids)
   100	    if not discovery_corpus.units:
   101	        # the discovery partition selects no actual corpus units — a hypothesis "discovered"
   102	        # from zero data is not data-surfaced at all; it would confirm cleanly on the held-out
   103	        # set and silently launder a hand-authored claim through Firewall B. Fail loud.
   104	        raise FirewallViolation(
   105	            "discovery partition selects no corpus units — nothing to discover from "
   106	            "(do split ids match the corpus unit ids?)"
   107	        )
   108	    hypotheses = list(discover(discovery_corpus))  # sees only the discovery partition
   109	
   110	    held_out = subset_corpus(corpus, split.confirm_ids)  # disjoint from discovery (split invariant)
   111	    if not held_out.units:
   112	        # the confirmation partition selects no actual corpus units — confirming on an empty
   113	        # held-out set is a vacuous test (the split ids don't match the corpus). Fail loud.
   114	        raise FirewallViolation(
   115	            "confirmation partition selects no corpus units — nothing to confirm on "
   116	            "(do split ids match the corpus unit ids?)"
   117	        )
   118	    verdicts: list[Verdict] = []
   119	    for hypothesis in hypotheses:
   120	        if hypothesis.origin is not HypothesisOrigin.DISCOVERY:
   121	            raise FirewallViolation(
   122	                f"hypothesis {hypothesis.hypothesis_id!r} is {hypothesis.origin.value!r}; the "
   123	                "discovery runner expects DISCOVERY-origin (data-surfaced) hypotheses"
   124	            )
   125	        # Pre-registration enforced by the runner: the proof must bind THIS hypothesis's content
   126	        # (no post-lock content swap) and its attested lock time must precede this confirmation.
   127	        require_preregistered(
   128	            hypothesis, authority=authority, not_after=_dt.datetime.now(tz=_dt.timezone.utc)
   129	        )
   130	        verdict = confirm(hypothesis, held_out)
   131	        if verdict.hypothesis_id != hypothesis.hypothesis_id:
   132	            raise FirewallViolation(
   133	                f"verdict reports hypothesis_id {verdict.hypothesis_id!r} for hypothesis "
   134	                f"{hypothesis.hypothesis_id!r} — hypothesis↔verdict misattribution"
   135	            )
   136	        verdicts.append(verdict)
   137	    return verdicts

     1	"""Typed, falsifiable hypotheses (METHODOLOGY.md §3).
     2	
     3	Every hypothesis names what is claimed, the test, the data it runs on, and a pre-specified
     4	decision rule. Two origins:
     5	
     6	- ``DISCOVERY`` — surfaced by the data, then made specific, locked, and timestamped before
     7	  confirmation (Firewall B).
     8	- ``EXTERNAL_CLAIM`` — derived by the adapter from an external expert claim, tested against
     9	  the blind baseline (Firewall A).
    10	
    11	Two kinds, which select the confirmation mechanism:
    12	
    13	- ``UNIT_LEVEL`` — confirmed on a held-out split.
    14	- ``WHOLE_CORPUS`` — no held-out object possible; confirmed against null/permutation
    15	  distributions and stability across resamples.
    16	"""
    17	
    18	from __future__ import annotations
    19	
    20	from dataclasses import dataclass, field
    21	from enum import Enum
    22	from typing import Any, Literal, Mapping
    23	
    24	from assay_engine._frozen import freeze_mapping
    25	
    26	# The tail a claim predicts the statistic lies in, relative to the null.
    27	Direction = Literal["greater", "less"]
    28	
    29	
    30	class HypothesisKind(Enum):
    31	    UNIT_LEVEL = "unit_level"
    32	    WHOLE_CORPUS = "whole_corpus"
    33	
    34	
    35	class HypothesisOrigin(Enum):
    36	    DISCOVERY = "discovery"
    37	    EXTERNAL_CLAIM = "external_claim"
    38	
    39	
    40	@dataclass(frozen=True, slots=True)
    41	class Hypothesis:
    42	    """A locked, falsifiable assertion ready for confirmatory testing.
    43	
    44	    ``locked_at`` and ``timestamp_proof`` are populated when the hypothesis is sealed for
    45	    pre-registration. An unlocked hypothesis (either field ``None``) must not be passed to a
    46	    confirmatory test.
    47	
    48	    Scope of ``locked`` (audit pass 1, issue #6): ``locked`` is only a cheap **presence**
    49	    predicate — both fields populated. It is NOT the methodology-grade check and carries no
    50	    cryptographic meaning on its own. The real pre-registration verification —
    51	    content-binding (the proof covers this hypothesis's content), timestamp attestation, and
    52	    lock-before-confirm ordering — lives in
    53	    :mod:`assay_engine.methodology.preregistration` (``verify_preregistration`` /
    54	    ``require_preregistered``), and the confirmatory runners (:mod:`adjudication`,
    55	    :mod:`discovery`) enforce it via a supplied ``TimestampAuthority``. Use
    56	    :func:`~assay_engine.methodology.preregistration.lock_hypothesis` to produce a hypothesis
    57	    whose lock actually verifies; a hand-set ``timestamp_proof`` string satisfies ``locked``
    58	    but will fail ``verify_preregistration``.
    59	    """
    60	
    61	    hypothesis_id: str
    62	    statement: str
    63	    kind: HypothesisKind
    64	    origin: HypothesisOrigin
    65	    test_name: str
    66	    decision_rule: str
    67	    source_claim_id: str | None = None
    68	    locked_at: str | None = None
    69	    timestamp_proof: str | None = None
    70	    # The predicted direction, fixed at pre-registration so it cannot be chosen post-hoc
    71	    # after seeing the baseline (audit pass 2, issue #24). For an EXTERNAL_CLAIM hypothesis
    72	    # the adapter derives this from the claim's assertion, not from the baseline.
    73	    predicted_direction: Direction | None = None
    74	    params: Mapping[str, Any] = field(default_factory=dict)
    75	
    76	    @property
    77	    def locked(self) -> bool:
    78	        return self.locked_at is not None and self.timestamp_proof is not None
    79	
    80	    def __post_init__(self) -> None:
    81	        if self.origin is HypothesisOrigin.EXTERNAL_CLAIM and not self.source_claim_id:
    82	            raise ValueError("EXTERNAL_CLAIM hypotheses must carry a source_claim_id")
    83	        object.__setattr__(self, "params", freeze_mapping(self.params))
########## _canonical.py ##########
     1	"""Type-faithful canonical hashing — the engine's single content-digest primitive.
     2	
     3	Two unrelated places need a *stable, type-faithful* content hash: the baseline determinism
     4	harness (so the same inputs reproduce the same baseline) and pre-registration (so a lock
     5	binds the exact hypothesis content). Both must distinguish values that merely share a
     6	``str()`` — ``int 1`` from ``str "1"``, ``date(2020,1,1)`` from ``"2020-01-01"`` — or the
     7	hash is a liability rather than a guarantee.
     8	
     9	This module is the one implementation, hoisted here so it sits *below* both
    10	``methodology`` and ``baseline`` in the import graph (neither may depend on the other). It is
    11	dependency-free and pure. Anything it cannot canonicalize reproducibly is refused **loudly**:
    12	a default (address-based) ``repr`` would make the digest differ every process and silently
    13	break the guarantee it exists to provide.
    14	"""
    15	
    16	from __future__ import annotations
    17	
    18	import datetime as _dt
    19	import decimal
    20	import hashlib
    21	import json
    22	import uuid
    23	from typing import Any, Mapping
    24	
    25	
    26	def keytag(key: Any) -> str:
    27	    """A type-faithful, sortable string for a mapping key, so distinct keys that happen to
    28	    share a ``str()`` (e.g. int ``1`` vs str ``"1"``) do NOT collide."""
    29	    return f"{type(key).__name__}::{key!r}"
    30	
    31	
    32	def canonical_plain(value: Any) -> Any:
    33	    """Canonicalize ``value`` into a fully JSON-native, type-faithful structure for hashing.
    34	
    35	    Mappings become a sorted list of ``[typed-key, value]`` pairs; bytes, sets, and any
    36	    non-JSON-native leaf are tagged with their type rather than stringified. Nothing is coerced
    37	    with ``str()``, so the content hash distinguishes values that share a ``str()``.
    38	    """
    39	    if value is None or isinstance(value, (str, bool, int, float)):
    40	        return value  # JSON-native scalars; json encodes 1, "1", true, 1.0 distinctly
    41	    if isinstance(value, bytes):
    42	        return {"__bytes__": value.hex()}
    43	    if isinstance(value, Mapping):
    44	        pairs = sorted(
    45	            ([keytag(k), canonical_plain(v)] for k, v in value.items()), key=lambda p: p[0]
    46	        )
    47	        return {"__map__": pairs}
    48	    if isinstance(value, (list, tuple)):
    49	        return [canonical_plain(v) for v in value]
    50	    if isinstance(value, (set, frozenset)):
    51	        return {"__set__": sorted((canonical_plain(v) for v in value), key=repr)}
    52	    # A small allowlist of leaf types with a value-faithful canonical form.
    53	    if isinstance(value, (_dt.date, _dt.datetime, _dt.time)):
    54	        return {"__temporal__": value.isoformat()}
    55	    if isinstance(value, decimal.Decimal):
    56	        return {"__decimal__": str(value)}
    57	    if isinstance(value, uuid.UUID):
    58	        return {"__uuid__": str(value)}
    59	    # Anything else is refused LOUDLY rather than repr-tagged: a default (address-based) repr
    60	    # would make the digest differ every process, silently breaking the guarantee.
    61	    raise TypeError(
    62	        f"cannot canonicalize a leaf of type "
    63	        f"{type(value).__module__}.{type(value).__qualname__!r} reproducibly — convert it to a "
    64	        "JSON-native value, bytes, date/datetime/Decimal/UUID before hashing"
    65	    )
    66	
    67	
    68	def canonical_json(value: Any) -> str:
    69	    """The canonical JSON text of ``value`` — stable across processes and key orderings."""
    70	    return json.dumps(canonical_plain(value), sort_keys=True, allow_nan=False)
    71	
    72	
    73	def hash_bytes(data: bytes) -> str:
    74	    return hashlib.sha256(data).hexdigest()
    75	
    76	
    77	def hash_text(text: str) -> str:
    78	    return hash_bytes(text.encode("utf-8"))
    79	
    80	
    81	def hash_value(value: Any) -> str:
    82	    """Stable, type-faithful content hash of an arbitrary value.
    83	
    84	    ``canonical_plain`` canonicalizes everything to JSON-native form first, so ``json.dumps``
    85	    needs no ``default=`` coercion — anything it still cannot encode is a real bug to surface,
    86	    not to silently stringify.
    87	    """
    88	    return hash_text(canonical_json(value))

content.text:
Good - no collision there because mappings get wrapped in `__map__`. The tagged forms are only produced for actual bytes/set, and a real dict gets `__map__`. That's safe.

Now let me look at the `_empirical_p` carefully. The concern: a `null_distribution` where observed is at the extreme could yield both `p_support` and `p_contra` small. But `_validate_alpha` requires alpha<0.5. Let me check: can both p_support and p_contra be <= alpha simultaneously? With +1 smoothing, p_support + p_contra can exceed 1 (overlapping count of `==observed`). Let me probe whether a contradiction can be wrongly reported when the claim is actually supported.

     1	"""Study runner — the composed, governed, end-to-end pipeline (ADR-0010).
     2	
     3	The phase machine (:mod:`assay_engine.orchestration.phases`) *names* the ordered stages a
     4	study moves through; the firewall runners enforce the methodology; the seams provide tracing,
     5	experiment tracking, data versioning, and (in the adapter's callables) reasoning. This module
     6	composes them into a single runnable flow, engine-owned so the ordering and the governance
     7	handoffs are guaranteed once, not re-implemented per study:

     9	    INGEST → BASELINE(blind, Firewall A) → [DISCOVERY → PREREGISTER → gate → CONFIRM (Firewall B)]
    10	           → [ADJUDICATE → SCORE (Firewall A, shared baseline)] → REPORT

    12	At every step it (a) records an append-only provenance entry *before the next step runs*
    13	(GOVERNANCE §3), (b) opens a trace span (observability seam), optionally (c) versions the source
    14	and logs to an experiment tracker, and (d) enforces the methodological invariants structurally:
    15	the baseline is built blind, the split ids are validated against the corpus, hypotheses are
    16	verifiably pre-registered before confirmation, a human gate reviews before *every* confirmatory
    17	step, claim-derived hypotheses are locked before the baseline exists, and the visited phase
    18	sequence must equal the mode's required sequence.

   115	    re-deriving the fingerprint from the recorded/scored claims gets the value the trail records.

Now I have a good picture. Let me focus on a few concrete things I noticed.

1. In `discover_and_confirm` (discovery.py), the hypotheses are confirmed but there's NO uniqueness check on hypothesis_ids — unlike the pipeline `run_study` which calls `_require_unique_ids`. So a study calling `discover_and_confirm` directly could get duplicate verdicts. But these are different entry points. Let me check whether `discover_and_confirm` is used by the pipeline. It's not (pipeline reimplements the loop). So `discover_and_confirm` is a public API path missing the dedup that pass-2's #138 added to the pipeline path. Let me verify.

2. The `_record_adjudication_step` records the "preregister" entry AFTER the verdict in `adjudicate_with_baseline` ordering — but in the on_step it records preregister then verdict. However the actual `require_preregistered` happens before `confirm`. The provenance ordering records preregister before verdict, but both are recorded only after confirm has run. That's a minor ordering issue but recorded in correct order.

Let me check the discovery double-dip more carefully: in `discover_and_confirm`, is there a check that confirmed hypotheses were actually derived from the discovery partition? And critically — the `subset_corpus` and Firewall B. Let me check `confirm_held_out` — does it verify `evaluated_ids` against the split? In the pipeline path, `confirm_held_out` is handed `held_out` corpus but the engine never calls `split.assert_confirm_only`. The study's confirmer is trusted. Let me look at the duplicate hypothesis check gap in discovery.py.

Now let me check a more serious methodology concern. The `not_after` in `discover_and_confirm` (discovery.py:128) uses `datetime.now()` captured *per hypothesis inside the loop, AFTER discover() runs*. But more importantly: in discovery, the `not_after` for require_preregistered is `now()` captured AFTER `discover()` already ran and locked the hypotheses. So the lock-before-confirm ordering is satisfied trivially. That's fine for discovery (discovery legitimately locks during discover).

But here's a real one: In `discover_and_confirm`, the hypotheses are confirmed but there is **no check that confirmation only touches held-out ids**. The engine hands `confirm` the `held_out` corpus, but Firewall B's `assert_confirm_only` is never invoked by the runner — it relies on the study's confirmer to call it. Same in pipeline. That's documented as study obligation. Let me not over-report that since it's the same custodial/signature-level scope documented.

Note `-0.0` and `0.0` produce different JSON (`-0.0` vs `0.0`) so they hash differently, but `-0.0 == 0.0`. That's a non-issue for content binding (it just means content is faithful). And `True` vs `1` differ in JSON. Good.

But wait — `canonical_plain` line 39: `isinstance(value, (str, bool, int, float))` returns the value directly. `True` (a bool) is returned as `True`, and json encodes it as `true`. `1` (int) returns `1`. So they differ. Good.

I notice `corpus_fingerprint` (determinism.py:44) sorts units by `unit_id` and relations by `(source_id, target_id, kind)`. If two units share the same `unit_id` (duplicate), the fingerprint sort is stable but doesn't detect the duplicate. More importantly, two distinct corpora with same content but different metadata produce different fingerprints — fine.

But here's a methodology concern about `corpus_fingerprint`: it does NOT incorporate the *number* of units distinctly from duplicate ids. If a corpus has duplicate unit_ids, the baseline could be built on N units but the fingerprint just hashes them all. Not a verdict corruption per se.

docs/hardening/PASS-1.md:123:- **Problem:** BaselineArtifact.__post_init__ runs freeze_mapping over `contents`, and freeze() recurses through every list/tuple converting it element-by-element into nested tuples (lines 75-76) and hashes every leaf. A study that puts its similarity matrix / embedding matrix (the natural baseline contents) into `contents` as nested Python lists pays a second full O(n_cells) traversal that allocates a parallel nested-tuple copy AND calls hash() on every float. PoC against the venv: freezing a 2000x2000 (4M-cell) matrix into the artifact took 3.29 s and 64 MB on top of the original. Combined with the previous finding, a 5k-embedding baseline that builds an in-memory similarity matrix is materialized at least three times (numpy array -> nested list -> nested tuple). Breaking point: ~3-5k units with any dense O(n^2) content; for raw embedding matrices (n x d) it scales with n*d and a 100k-doc x 768-dim embedding set (77M cells) is hundreds of MB-to-GB of duplicated tuples plus tens of seconds of pure-Python work at artifact construction.
docs/hardening/PASS-1.md:292:- **Problem:** upsert builds the complete `points` list — one PointStruct per id, each holding `list(v)` (a fresh copy of the whole vector) — for the entire input before the chunking loop at line 88 sends anything. For a real ingest (e.g. 5M docs x 768 float dims) this is the entire embedding set re-copied into Python objects in RAM simultaneously, which on a single Mac mini node is the OOM path the batch loop appears to defend against. ids/vectors are typed as Sequence (already materialized), so the call site has likely loaded everything too, but the upsert needlessly doubles it.

content.text:
Confirmed: no dedup test for `discover_and_confirm`, and it is publicly exported from `assay_engine.methodology`. This is a real, public-API incomplete-fix.

Now let me solidify the second finding (hypothesis_id uniqueness in adjudication). Pass-2 #15 problem statement explicitly mentions "or the resulting hypothesis_ids" and "Identity (claim<->hypothesis<->verdict)". The verdict accounting is per-hypothesis-derived-verdict; the dedup only checks the *input* claim_id. A buggy `hypothesis_for` mapping distinct claims to one hypothesis_id is exactly the "claim↔hypothesis misattribution" family the runner otherwise guards (it checks source_claim_id match per claim, but two claims can't both have source_claim_id matching one hypothesis... wait).

Let me re-examine: the runner checks `hypothesis.source_claim_id != claim.claim_id` raises. So for claim c1, hypothesis_for must return a hypothesis with source_claim_id='c1'; for c2, source_claim_id='c2'. But both can share hypothesis_id='HSAME' (source_claim_id differs, hypothesis_id same). The digest includes hypothesis_id AND source_claim_id, so the two locked hypotheses have DIFFERENT digests/proofs but the SAME hypothesis_id. Both pass require_preregistered. The verdicts both report hypothesis_id='HSAME'. Scorecard counts both. That's the genuine residual gap. My PoC confirmed it (supported=2, ids both HSAME).

This is lower severity than the discover_and_confirm gap because it requires a buggy hypothesis_for that reuses ids across claims, but it directly corrupts the scorecard the same way #15 described. Let me write up findings.

Let me do a final scan for one more potential issue in the verdict_from_pvalue `powered=False` overriding a contradiction. If `not powered` returns indeterminate even when significant in opposite tail — that's intended (underpowered → can't decide). OK.

Let me check one thing on the `confirm_whole_corpus` contradiction path: it returns contradicted even when `not powered` isn't a concept there. There's no `powered` arg for whole_corpus. The contradiction is reported regardless of stability — documented. Fine.

I have two solid findings. Let me finalize.

findings.title: discover_and_confirm (public Firewall-B runner) has no hypothesis_id uniqueness guard — incomplete fix of pass-2 #15/#138
findings.defect: Pass-2 finding #15 (fix #138) identified that within a run, duplicate identities inflate verdict/scorecard accounting, and its own analysis explicitly recorded (docs/hardening/PASS-2.md:219) that `discover_and_confirm` "has the identical gap for duplicate hypothesis_ids returned by `discover`", with the suggested fix saying to "track hypothesis_ids in the discovery loop." The landed fix only added a claim_id guard to adjudicate_with_baseline and a hypothesis_id guard to the *pipeline* discovery loop (pipeline.py:452). The standalone, publicly-exported discover_and_confirm runner (exported from assay_engine.methodology, __init__.py:22,61) was never given the guard. A study's `discover` step that returns the same hypothesis_id twice (an adapter bug, or a hand-authored claim laundered as two identical candidates) produces two verdicts attributed to one hypothesis, double-counting it in any downstream tally the caller builds from the returned verdict list. This is the exact within-run identity-uniqueness invariant #138 was meant to close, missed on one of the three call sites the original finding named.
findings.poc: Reproduced against .venv. corpus u0..u3, split disc={u0,u1}/conf={u2,u3}; discover() returns [h, h] for one locked hypothesis H1; confirm returns Verdict.supported. discover_and_confirm(...) returns 2 verdicts with ids ['H1','H1'] — no FirewallViolation. (The pipeline path with the same duplicate is rejected by _require_unique_ids; this standalone runner is not.)
findings.suggested_fix: Mirror pipeline.py's guard inside discover_and_confirm: after `hypotheses = list(discover(...))`, raise FirewallViolation on any repeated hypothesis_id (reuse the same logic as pipeline._require_unique_ids / adjudication's seen-set). Add a test in tests/test_discovery.py asserting a duplicate hypothesis_id is rejected, paralleling test_pipeline.py::test_duplicate_claim_ids_are_rejected.

findings.title: Adjudication scorecard dedups input claim_id only, not the resulting hypothesis_id — distinct claims mapped to one hypothesis_id inflate the scorecard
findings.location: src/assay_engine/methodology/adjudication.py:199-235 (adjudicate_with_baseline; seen_claim_ids only) and src/assay_engine/pipeline.py:527 (_require_unique_ids on claim_id only, adjudication mode)
findings.defect: Pass-2 #15's problem statement (docs/hardening/PASS-2.md:213) flagged that uniqueness must cover "claim_ids (or the resulting hypothesis_ids)" and that identity is claim<->hypothesis<->verdict. The landed #138 fix enforces uniqueness only on the *input* claim_id, in both adjudicate_with_baseline (seen_claim_ids set) and the pipeline (_require_unique_ids over claim_id). The scorecard, however, is verdict-accounting keyed by the *hypothesis* the verdict answers. A buggy hypothesis_for that maps two distinct claims (c1, c2) onto the same hypothesis_id (with correct, differing source_claim_id values so the claim<->hypothesis match check passes, and differing digests/proofs so each pre-registers cleanly) yields two verdicts both reporting that one hypothesis_id. _score counts both, inflating n_supported/total and biasing alignment_rate — the §5 'score the source' metric — with no error. This is the residual half of #15 that its own write-up named but the fix did not close: hypothesis-level identity uniqueness in adjudication.
findings.poc: Reproduced against .venv. claims=[c1,c2] (distinct claim_ids), hypothesis_for returns a hypothesis with hypothesis_id='HSAME' and source_claim_id=claim.claim_id for each, each locked via LocalHmacAuthority. adjudicate_with_baseline(...) returns a SourceScorecard with n_supported=2 and verdict ids ['HSAME','HSAME'] — the single hypothesis counted twice. The claim_id guard does not fire (claim_ids are distinct); the source_claim_id match check does not fire (each matches its own claim).
findings.suggested_fix: In adjudicate_with_baseline, also track seen hypothesis_ids (and optionally seen source_claim_ids) and raise FirewallViolation on a repeat — identity must be unique at the hypothesis level, not only the claim level, because the scorecard is hypothesis-keyed. Mirror the same check in the pipeline adjudication path. Add a test asserting two distinct claims producing the same hypothesis_id are rejected.

content.text:
I analyzed the methodology-integrity core of hc-assay (the two firewalls, pre-registration content-binding + timestamp ordering, the measurement/interpretation fence, the three-verdict statistics, scorecard accounting, and the provenance hash-chain). The cryptographic and statistical machinery is genuinely well-hardened — content binding, the empirical-p both-tails impossibility under alpha<0.5 (verified), the canonical serializer's tagged-form collision resistance (verified no `__map__`/`__bytes__`/`__set__` collision), the HMAC chain, and the lock-before-confirm ordering all hold up under probing.

I found two real defects, both incomplete fixes of pass-2 finding #15/#138 (within-run identity uniqueness), with working PoCs against the venv:

1. MEDIUM — `discover_and_confirm` (`src/assay_engine/methodology/discovery.py:118-136`), the publicly-exported standalone Firewall-B runner, has no hypothesis_id uniqueness guard. Pass-2's own analysis (`docs/hardening/PASS-2.md:219`) named this exact call site as having "the identical gap," but the landed fix only covered `adjudicate_with_baseline` and the pipeline discovery loop. PoC: `discover()` returning the same locked hypothesis twice yields two verdicts `['H1','H1']` with no error.

2. MEDIUM — Adjudication accounting (`src/assay_engine/methodology/adjudication.py:199-235` and `src/assay_engine/pipeline.py:527`) dedups the input `claim_id` only, not the resulting `hypothesis_id`. The scorecard is hypothesis-keyed, so a buggy `hypothesis_for` mapping two distinct claims onto one hypothesis_id inflates `n_supported`/`total` and biases `alignment_rate`. PoC: two distinct claims → `hypothesis_id='HSAME'` → scorecard `n_supported=2`, ids `['HSAME','HSAME']`, no FirewallViolation. Pass-2 #15's problem statement explicitly called for covering "the resulting hypothesis_ids"; only the claim_id half landed.

Both are returned in the StructuredOutput tool call.
